none: A woman tailors an outfit on a model, carrying a needle between her teeth.

body.creator: Ada
body.value: Furthermore, the two have swapped roles in another key way. As Amy Greenhough notes, “When Alma says that Reynolds has made her ‘dreams come true’ (Anderson 0:01:13–0:01:14) she echoes the Disneyfied idea of the fairy-tale ending, but unlike ‘Beauty, the heroine, as the questor who discovers [the Beast’s] true nature’ (Warner 275) and finds him permanently transformed by her love, Alma must force Reynolds’s transformation over and over again into the man she loves. In the final moments of the film she describes the potential for the inflicted sickness to kill him, but smiling as she does so, she reveals that the real transformation of the film is hers: she is now a Beast too” (Greenhough, “Spinning Bodies into Silk,” 203).

body.creator: Ada
body.value: Adam Nayman argues that “Alma is not trying to fix Reynold’s business, or to usurp his role as its figurehead and resident genius... Nor is she really trying to supplant Cyril... Alma doesn’t want to lead, just to stay, and, more to the point, to be ‘Mrs. Woodcock’ in both senses of the word—to be a hybrid wife and mother” (Nayman, Masterworks, 224).  That may be the case, but tellingly, Alma has supplanted Reynolds in this vision of their future together. They’re together when dropping off their child with Cyril at a park, but in the house, he’s nowhere to be seen. Here, she persons the same actions he has throughout the film, pinning pieces of fabric and evaluating fits. Alma even dresses this unknown woman—whose face is never shown—in a dress that is similar in color to the ones that Reynolds has made for her throughout the film and that she wore during their first two meetings. The dresses likewise replace their physical offspring in this sequence of future moments that Alma envisions. For Alma, true union with Reynolds means being able to craft together, to bring their aesthetics to fruition together.
